hub_subtitle: Business English para Finanças Corporativas, Entrevistas e Carreira Internacional

lesson.menu_title: Holding the Room -- High-Stakes Financial Presentations
lesson.menu_desc: Conduzir uma apresentação financeira sob pressão + signposting language -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 11 -- Holding the Room
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Felipe Pimenta | Lesson 11 -- Holding the Room

listenings.text: Let me tell you the difference between a CFO who presents and a CFO who holds the room, because it is not the numbers. Both of them have the same numbers. The difference is that one of them tells you where you are at every moment, and the other one makes you guess. Here is what I do now, and I did not do it for the first ten years. In the first thirty seconds I give the agenda, out loud, and I give the headline number, out loud, before anything else. I do not build up to it. I do not save it for the end like a magician. I say, the bottom line is this, we are two million below plan, here is why, and here is the fix, and I will drill down into each of those in turn. And then, and this is the part people miss, when a hard question comes in the middle, I do not panic and answer it badly. I say, that is exactly the right question, let me circle back to it in two minutes when the number in front of it will make it easier to answer. And they let me, every time, because I told them where we were going. Control of a room is not confidence. It is signposting.

I coach executives on presentations, and I am going to tell you the single most common way that brilliant finance people lose a board. They gloss over the one number they are frightened of. They speed up, they lower their voice, they bury it in the middle of a slide full of other figures, and they hope. And every experienced director in that room can smell it, because the energy changes for exactly four seconds. So here is the rule I give them. Name the caveat before anyone asks for it. Slow down on the weak number, do not speed up. Say, I want to flag one figure here that deserves a proper caveat, and then give it, cleanly, with the reason. Two things happen. First, you look like the most senior person in the room, because only the senior ones volunteer the bad news. And second, you take the weapon out of the hands of the person who was about to raise it. A presentation is not a performance of confidence. It is a map you hand to the room, and the best maps mark the swamp, they do not hide it.

items.situation: "You open the board meeting. Where do you start?"
tips: The bottom line is this: we are two million below plan. Let me give you the agenda, then drill down.
Headline number first. Never build up to it like a magician.

items.situation: "A director interrupts with a hard question, mid-slide."
tips: That is exactly the right question. Let me circle back to it in two minutes.
Park it out loud. Do not panic-answer and lose your thread.

items.situation: "You reach the number you are frightened of."
tips: I want to flag one figure here that deserves a proper caveat.
Slow down on the weak number. Never speed up and gloss over it.

items.situation: "Someone asks for a figure you do not have exactly."
tips: In ballpark terms it is around three million; I will confirm the exact figure and circle back.
Give the ballpark, promise the exact number. Never invent one.

items.situation: "You have two minutes left and three slides to go."
tips: Let me skip the detail and give you the recap: three things matter tonight.
Signpost the cut. Do not sprint silently through slides.

items.situation: "The chair says: close it for us."
tips: To sum up: the headline number, the one caveat, and the fix. That is the whole story.
A clean recap of exactly three things. Then stop talking.
